title: Te Taiao o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku / The Ecosystem of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream flowing through our rohe that features in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about the journey of Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and its current state reflects both historical land use and contemporary environmental pressures.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data (pH, nitrate levels, turbidity) from three sites along Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku using scientific equipment
Compare scientific water quality data with mātauranga Māori indicators of stream health (presence of native species, clarity, smell) documented through observation
Create a collaborative report that integrates scientific findings and cultural perspectives to assess the overall health of the awa and propose one evidence-based action for improvement

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge the awa. A local kaumātua shares the pūrākau of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku and traditional knowledge about indicators of a healthy stream. Students make initial observations and sketch the stream environment.

activities.title: Ngā Tohu Pūtaiao - Scientific Data Collection
activities.description: In small groups, students rotate through testing stations at designated sites along the awa. They use water testing kits to measure and record pH, nitrate levels, dissolved oxygen, and turbidity, following precise scientific protocols.

activities.title: Ngā Tohu Māori - Cultural Data Collection
activities.description: Using guided observation sheets developed with local knowledge holders, students document cultural indicators of health: presence/absence of tuna (eels), kōura (crayfish), and manu (birds); clarity and smell of water; health of riparian plants like harakeke.

activities.title: Titiro Whakamuri, Kōkiri Whakamua
activities.description: Students analyze and graph their collected data. They compare scientific measurements with cultural observations to form an integrated picture of the stream's health, identifying potential causes for any issues found (e.g., rural runoff, erosion).

activities.title: Te Whakatau - The Decision
activities.description: Groups synthesize their findings to propose one actionable solution (e.g., planting natives for bank stabilization, creating a community education campaign). They present their evidence and proposal to the class.

resources: Hanna Instruments HI98129 pHep®5 pH/Temperature Tester or similar water testing kits
Local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi environmental team
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and guides
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku - The Stream of the Tree Fuchsia' and local pūrākau

tasks: Completed field data sheets showing accurate scientific measurements and cultural observations
A collaboratively written scientific report (group) that includes: data tables/graphs, analysis comparing scientific and cultural data, and a justified proposal for action
A brief individual reflection on the process of integrating mātauranga Māori and Western science

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science - Students will explore how scientific and Māori knowledge systems can interact and inform each other.
Living World: Ecology - Investigate the impact of natural events and human actions on a New Zealand ecosystem.
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Develop an understanding of how water cycles through and connects environments.
